video_title: Wellum’s Warning: The Tech Boom Is Starting to Crack
gist: Value investor Jonathan Wellum warns that the AI/semiconductor/data-center boom shows classic late-1990s bubble traits—parabolic moves, sky-high expectations, FOMO and an IPO parade where insiders sell at the top—and urges patience, higher discount rates for unproven tech, and exposure only via cash-rich, moaty leaders like Apple, Alphabet and Amazon at sensible prices.

insights.claim: Wellum says the semiconductor index rising over 70% year-to-date with parabolic moves and an endless flow of capital into AI and data centers is classic over-investment that warrants stepping back.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: A 70%+ run in a single sub-index is a momentum-driven signal to trim or avoid chasing, not to add.
insights.quote: When you start to see a sub indices like the semiconductor index go up, you know, 70 plus percent year-to-date... this is classic over investing.

insights.claim: Wellum draws a direct parallel to 1999-2000, noting the Nasdaq peaked at 5100, fell 78%, and did not recover that level until 2015—15 years later.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Overpaying for a genuine technology revolution can still mean a 15-year round trip, so entry price and timing dominate long-run returns.
insights.quote: the Nasdaq hit 5100, but then it dropped 78%. 78%. It didn't get back to 5100 until 2015. That's 15 years later.

insights.claim: Wellum argues today's market repeats the dot-com pattern: explosive infrastructure capex frenzy, sky-high valuations, concentration in a few companies, speculative FOMO, and total uncertainty around earnings.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Gives a reusable five-point checklist to gauge how bubble-like the current AI cycle is.
insights.quote: you see explosive infrastructure capex frenzy at that time. You saw sky-high valuations. You saw market concentration in few companies. Speculative fever, FOMO, I can't miss out.

insights.claim: Wellum invokes survivor bias: many companies hyped in the internet bubble disappeared or were bought out cheaply, and the same culling will hit smaller AI players this cycle.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Today's leaderboard understates the failure rate; second-tier 'me-too' AI names carry hidden wipeout risk.
insights.quote: there's a lot of companies that were in that internet bubble that did not survive. They're gone.

insights.claim: Wellum warns the 'picks and shovels' thesis is not automatically safe, citing that many companies that laid internet fiber also went bankrupt.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Infrastructure-provider exposure to AI (data centers, networking) is not a free pass and still needs valuation discipline.
insights.quote: the people who laid all the fiber for the internet, a lot of them disappeared, too.

insights.claim: Wellum cautions that major IPOs have a poor short-run record, with some studies showing them down as much as 40-48% in the months after listing.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Buying the current IPO parade at the print historically risks a 40%+ near-term drawdown.
insights.quote: if you take a lot of the major IPOs, they'll be down as much as 40 and you know, and 48% over the next number of months afterwards.

insights.claim: Wellum stresses 'the seller knows more than the buyer'—insiders bring companies public at optimal, high valuations, so the IPO is the exit liquidity for early private investors.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Treat retail IPO buying as taking the other side of informed insiders cashing out near a local top.
insights.quote: the seller knows more than the buyer... the sellers usually pick the time that is optimal for them. And so they are picking a high.

insights.claim: Wellum reframes the risk in modern mega/late-stage IPOs: it is less about the business failing and more about sky-high baked-in expectations causing a 3-4 year capital-loss setback.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Even profitable market leaders can deliver multi-year losses if priced for perfection.
insights.quote: the sky-high expectations that are baked into these companies... Will you take a capital loss and be have a set setback for maybe 3 to 4 years?

insights.claim: Wellum flags post-IPO lockup expiry and selling pressure, plus governance and regulatory risk, as near-term overhangs on newly public AI and space names.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Lockup unlocks add a mechanical supply overhang on top of valuation risk for recent listings.
insights.quote: Post IPO selling pressure should be interesting also. The lockup dynamics of some of these ones.

insights.claim: Wellum says unproven, fast-changing tech businesses should be penalized with a higher discount rate (12-13% vs. ~7-8% for a stable name like Coca-Cola), which lowers present value even under optimistic cash-flow forecasts.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Properly risk-adjusting AI cash flows mathematically caps what you should pay—something the market is currently ignoring.
insights.quote: you're going to use a discount rate that might be 12, 13%... you bring down the present value of those cash flows even if you're optimistic.

insights.claim: Wellum uses an 'elevator to floor 100' analogy: you don't need to board at floor 1-4; waiting to get on at floor 20 with more clarity lowers risk while preserving most of the upside.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Patience and a later, clearer entry can capture most of a structural-growth story with far less downside.
insights.quote: if you're going to jump on an elevator and it's going to 100 floor, you don't necessarily have to jump on... floor 1 2 3 4. You can get on at floor 20.

insights.claim: Wellum warns against the 'cigar butt' value trap: a cheap stock can stay cheap and go nowhere for years, so low price alone is not value—predictability and durable moats matter more.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Screening for cheapness without quality and a catalyst risks dead money; some momentum or a takeout angle helps.
insights.quote: you can get into value traps also. So, a company could be trading at a low valuation. It might trade at a low valuation for a long time and go nowhere.

insights.claim: Wellum is openly skeptical that AI's productivity payoff will be as large or fast as assumed, noting buyers can cancel subscriptions and some firms have already burned their full AI budget in four to five months and are cutting back.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: If enterprise AI spend proves discretionary and ROI underwhelms, projected revenue/growth rates for AI vendors are too high.
insights.quote: some of the companies already have spent a lot and they're saying, 'Hey, wait a second. We spent our whole budget in the first four or five months of the year on AI and we got to cut back.'

insights.claim: Wellum notes value investing can include tech, citing Buffett/Berkshire's shift into Apple and Alphabet because Apple behaves like a consumer-staple with a deep, sticky ecosystem moat and large free cash flow.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Moaty, cash-generative tech leaders are the value-compatible way to get AI/tech exposure without speculation.
insights.quote: it's really a consumer almost a consumer staple business. I mean every we have to have a phone... So it's got a moat around it.

insights.claim: Wellum holds Amazon and Apple as his larger tech positions and frames Amazon's 2022-23 ~40-45% drawdown as a buying opportunity, since margin compression was reinvestment to grab share at high returns on equity.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Big drawdowns in dominant compounders that are reinvesting (not deteriorating) are entry points, not exits.
insights.quote: even in 2022 in '23... Amazon gave up about 40 45% of its value... It continued to grow every quarter.

insights.claim: Wellum likes MercadoLibre as a deeply undervalued Amazon-like leader dominating South American e-commerce and financial services, where Amazon has had little success.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: A local-knowledge regional monopolist reinvesting for dominance is a value play insulated from US mega-cap crowding.
insights.quote: we have a small company... the leader in South America, Mercado Libre... we think that company's trading at a very low valuation.

insights.claim: Wellum owns Sprott Inc. as a way to play the commodity space via the fee-earning parent (not the individual ETFs) and views it as a strong-brand takeout candidate.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Investing at the asset-manager parent captures aggregated fee revenue and an M&A optionality kicker on commodity exposure.
insights.quote: your best place to invest when you're investing in an asset manager is at the head... the parent company which then has all of the fee based revenue.

insights.claim: Wellum is enthusiastic about SpaceX's long-term potential (space data centers, asteroid mining) but says it is futuristic and risky, citing the Blue Origin rocket explosion, and would wait for a better entry rather than buy in now.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Wellum
insights.so_what: Even high-conviction visionary stories need a valuation-disciplined, patient entry given execution and timeline risk.
insights.quote: these are all very much futuristic and are going to take some time. And there's lots of things that can go wrong also along the way.
